name: Idunn
description: Idunn, often anglicized as Ithunn, is a vital figure within the Norse pantheon, primarily recognized as the keeper of the apples of immortality. These apples are not merely symbolic; they are the source of the gods' enduring youth and vigor. Without Idunn and her precious fruit, the Aesir would age and weaken, rendering them vulnerable. Her role therefore underscores the cyclical nature of life, death, and renewal, a recurring theme in Norse cosmology. Idunn's abduction by the giant Thiazi, facilitated by Loki's trickery, is a pivotal narrative demonstrating the fragility of the gods' immortality and the critical importance of Idunn's role. The subsequent decline in the Aesir's health highlights her irreplaceable function within the divine order. She is often associated with spring and rejuvenation, embodying the life-giving forces that sustain both gods and the world. Her name is believed to mean 'the rejuvenating one' or 'ever young'.
subtitle: Keeper of the Apples of Youth

keyMyths.title: Idunn's Abduction by Thiazi
keyMyths.description: The giant Thiazi, with Loki's forced assistance, kidnaps Idunn and steals her apples of youth. The Aesir begin to weaken and age, realizing the gravity of Idunn's absence. Desperate, they force Loki to retrieve her. Loki transforms into a falcon, rescues Idunn (transformed into a nut), and flees. Thiazi, in eagle form, pursues them, but is ultimately slain by the Aesir upon reaching Asgard. This myth highlights Idunn's essential role in maintaining the gods' vitality.

keyMyths.title: The Importance of the Apples
keyMyths.description: The apples of Idunn are the sole source of the Aesir's immortality. While not explicitly detailed in every narrative, the constant renewal of the gods' youth is implicitly tied to her possession and distribution of these magical fruits. They are not merely sustenance but a mystical conduit to sustained vitality, ensuring the Aesir's ability to uphold their duties and defend against the forces of chaos. The loss of these apples would mean the downfall of the gods.

keyMyths.title: Idunn as a Symbol of Spring
keyMyths.description: Although not explicitly stated in all sources, Idunn's association with apples and rejuvenation aligns her with the themes of spring and rebirth. Just as the earth awakens after winter, Idunn's apples provide the Aesir with renewed vigor. This connection reinforces the cyclical nature of Norse cosmology, linking the divine realm with the natural world. Her role as a guardian of youth implicitly connects her to the life-giving forces that drive the seasons.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations based on comparative mythology and symbolism

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, detailing Idunn's role as the keeper of the apples of youth and the story of her abduction.
